---
title: "Age-Adjusted Cardiovascular Risk and Hippocampal-Cognitive Coupling Along the Alzheimer's Disease Continuum"
subtitle: "Disentangling FRS Age-Weighting from True CVR Effects"
author:
  - name: Sofia Fernandez-Lozano
    affiliations:
      - ref: bic
      - ref: ipn
  - name: Sylvia Villeneuve
    affiliations:
      - ref: bic
      - ref: psych
      - ref: stopad
  - name: Louis D. Collins
    affiliations:
      - ref: bic
      - ref: ipn
      - ref: bbme
    email: louis.collins@mcgill.ca
    corresponding: true
affiliations:
  - id: bic
    name: McConnell Brain Imaging Centre
    department: Montreal Neurological Institute
    city: Montreal
    state: Quebec
    country: Canada
  - id: ipn
    name: McGill University
    department: Department of Neurology and Neurosurgery
    city: Montreal
    state: Quebec
    country: Canada
  - id: psych
    name: McGill University
    department: Department of Psychiatry
    city: Montreal
    state: Quebec
    country: Canada
  - id: stopad
    name: Douglas Mental Health Institute Research Centre
    department: StoP-AD Centre
    city: Montreal
    state: Quebec
    country: Canada
  - id: bbme
    name: McGill University
    department: Department of Biomedical Engineering
    city: Montreal
    state: Quebec
    country: Canada
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    documentclass: article
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    template-partials:
      - title.tex
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{tikz}
        \usetikzlibrary{shapes.geometric, arrows.meta, positioning}
        \usepackage{authblk}
        \renewcommand\Authfont{\normalsize}
        \renewcommand\Affilfont{\small\itshape}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{longtable}
        \usepackage{array}
        \usepackage{multirow}
        \usepackage{float}
        \floatplacement{table}{H}
        \floatplacement{figure}{H}
        \usepackage{placeins}
        \usepackage{textcomp}
        \usepackage{newunicodechar}
        \newunicodechar{≤}{\ensuremath{\leq}}
        \newunicodechar{≥}{\ensuremath{\geq}}
        \newunicodechar{×}{\ensuremath{\times}}
        \newunicodechar{β}{\ensuremath{\beta}}
        \newunicodechar{η}{\ensuremath{\eta}}
        \newunicodechar{χ}{\ensuremath{\chi}}
        \newunicodechar{σ}{\ensuremath{\sigma}}
        \newunicodechar{τ}{\ensuremath{\tau}}
        \newunicodechar{γ}{\ensuremath{\gamma}}
        \newunicodechar{ζ}{\ensuremath{\zeta}}
        \newunicodechar{φ}{\ensuremath{\varphi}}
        \newunicodechar{μ}{\ensuremath{\mu}}
        \newunicodechar{ν}{\ensuremath{\nu}}
        \newunicodechar{λ}{\ensuremath{\lambda}}
        \newunicodechar{Δ}{\ensuremath{\Delta}}
        \newunicodechar{ε}{\ensuremath{\varepsilon}}
        \newunicodechar{ρ}{\ensuremath{\rho}}
        \newunicodechar{→}{\ensuremath{\rightarrow}}
        \newunicodechar{↔}{\ensuremath{\leftrightarrow}}
        \newunicodechar{≈}{\ensuremath{\approx}}
        \newunicodechar{²}{\textsuperscript{2}}
        \setlength{\LTleft}{0pt}
        \setlength{\LTright}{\fill}
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    dev: cairo_pdf
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false

# Find project root and change to it so here() works
orig_wd <- getwd()
project_root <- normalizePath(
  file.path(orig_wd, "..")
)
if (!file.exists(
  file.path(project_root, ".here")
)) {
  project_root <- orig_wd
}
setwd(project_root)

library(here)
library(data.table)
library(gt)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
library(patchwork)

source(here("R/utils/config.R"))
source(here("R/utils/data_io.R"))
source(here("R/utils/tables.R"))
source(here("R/utils/plotting.R"))
source(here("R/utils/consort_diagram.R"))

config <- load_config()

# -------------------------------------------------
# Load pre-computed manuscript environment
# -------------------------------------------------
env.lst <- readRDS(
  here("outputs/manuscript_env.rds")
)
list2env(env.lst, envir = environment())
rm(env.lst)

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# gt table rendering for LaTeX/PDF output
# -------------------------------------------------------------------

knit_print.gt_tbl <- function(x, ...) {
  if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
    latex_code <- as.character(gt::as_latex(x))

    # Fix 1: Table width
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "\\\\begin\\{tabular\\*\\}\\{\\\\linewidth\\}\\{@\\{\\\\extracolsep\\{\\\\fill\\}\\}",
      "\\\\begin{tabular}{",
      latex_code
    )
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "\\\\end\\{tabular\\*\\}", "\\\\end{tabular}",
      latex_code
    )

    # Fix 2: HTML tags to LaTeX
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "<em>([^<]+)</em>", "\\\\textit{\\1}", latex_code
    )
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "<strong>([^<]+)</strong>", "\\\\textbf{\\1}", latex_code
    )
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "<sub>([^<]+)</sub>", "\\\\textsubscript{\\1}", latex_code
    )
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "<sup>([^<]+)</sup>", "\\\\textsuperscript{\\1}", latex_code
    )

    # Fix 3: Unicode symbols to LaTeX math
    latex_code <- gsub("\u0394", "$\\\\Delta$", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub("\u03B2", "$\\\\beta$", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub("\u03BB", "$\\\\lambda$", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub("\u03C3", "$\\\\sigma$", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub("\u03B7", "$\\\\eta$", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub("\u03C7", "$\\\\chi$", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub("\u03C4", "$\\\\tau$", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub("\u03B3", "$\\\\gamma$", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "\u00B2", "\\\\textsuperscript{2}", latex_code
    )
    latex_code <- gsub("\u2264", "$\\\\leq$", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub("\u2265", "$\\\\geq$", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub("\u2260", "$\\\\neq$", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub("\u00D7", "$\\\\times$", latex_code)

    # Fix 4: Unicode text commands missing {} terminator
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "\\\\(texttimes|texttwosuperior|texttwosuperiorp|textendash|textemdash)([A-Za-z])",
      "\\\\\\1{}\\2", latex_code
    )

    # Fix 5: Footnote minipage width
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "\\\\begin\\{minipage\\}\\{\\\\linewidth\\}",
      "\\\\begin{minipage}[t]{0.8\\\\textwidth}\\\\raggedright\\\\small",
      latex_code
    )

    # Fix 6: Column alignment (first left, rest right)
    fix_alignment <- function(code) {
      pattern <- "(\\\\begin\\{(?:tabular|longtable)\\}\\{)([lcr]+)(\\})"
      m <- gregexpr(pattern, code, perl = TRUE)
      if (m[[1]][1] == -1) return(code)
      matches <- regmatches(code, m)[[1]]
      for (match in matches) {
        col_spec <- gsub(".*\\{([lcr]+)\\}$", "\\1", match)
        n_cols <- nchar(col_spec)
        if (n_cols > 1) {
          new_spec <- paste0(
            "l", paste(rep("r", n_cols - 1), collapse = "")
          )
          new_match <- gsub(
            "\\{[lcr]+\\}$", paste0("{", new_spec, "}"), match
          )
          code <- sub(match, new_match, code, fixed = TRUE)
        }
      }
      code
    }
    latex_code <- fix_alignment(latex_code)

    # Fix 7: Table spacing
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "\\\\begin\\{tabular\\}",
      "\\\\renewcommand{\\\\arraystretch}{1.15}\\\\begin{tabular}",
      latex_code
    )

    # Fix 8: Escape [ ] in table cells
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "\\\\addlinespace\\[([^]]+)\\]",
      "<<ADDLNSP_\\1_>>", latex_code
    )
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "\\\\\\\\\\[([0-9.]+pt)\\]",
      "<<LINEBRK_\\1_>>", latex_code
    )
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "\\\\begin\\{minipage\\}\\[([a-z])\\]",
      "<<MINIPAGE_\\1_>>", latex_code
    )
    latex_code <- gsub("\\[", "{[}", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub("\\]", "{]}", latex_code)
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "<<ADDLNSP_([^_]+)_>>",
      "\\\\addlinespace[\\1]", latex_code
    )
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "<<LINEBRK_([^_]+)_>>",
      "\\\\\\\\[\\1]", latex_code
    )
    latex_code <- gsub(
      "<<MINIPAGE_([a-z])_>>",
      "\\\\begin{minipage}[\\1]", latex_code
    )

    knitr::asis_output(latex_code)
  } else {
    gt:::knit_print.gt_tbl(x, ...)
  }
}
registerS3method(
  "knit_print", "gt_tbl", knit_print.gt_tbl,
  envir = asNamespace("knitr")
)

# Domain labels for cognitive measures
DOMAIN_LABELS <- c(
  MEM = "Memory", LAN = "Language",
  EXF = "Executive Function"
)

# Helper: Format-aware markdown (handles LaTeX vs HTML)
fmt_crossformat <- function(gt_tbl, columns, rows = everything()) {
  if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
    gt::fmt(
      gt_tbl, columns = {{ columns }},
      rows = {{ rows }}, fns = function(x) {
        x <- gsub("\\*([^*]+)\\*", "\\\\textit{\\1}", x)
        x <- gsub("~([^~]+)~", "\\\\textsubscript{\\1}", x)
        x
      }
    )
  } else {
    gt::fmt_markdown(
      gt_tbl, columns = {{ columns }}, rows = {{ rows }}
    )
  }
}
```

---

# Abstract {.unnumbered}

**INTRODUCTION:**
The Framingham Risk Score (FRS) is widely used to
index cardiovascular risk (CVR) in aging research,
yet its heavy age-weighting may confound
associations with brain and cognitive outcomes.

**METHODS:**
In `r n_subjects` amyloid-positive ADNI participants,
we compared FRS with an age-adjusted CVR measure
(CVR~mimic~) from a MIMIC model. Sex-stratified
LME 3-way interaction models and LGCM mediation
models tested CVR effects on hippocampal-cognitive
trajectories.

**RESULTS:**
FRS predicted hippocampal atrophy rate
(CVR → HVR slope significant in
`r abs_stats$frs_a_sig`/6 LGCM mediation
models); CVR~mimic~ did not
(`r abs_stats$cvr_a_sig`/6). HVR-cognitive
coupling was significant in
`r abs_stats$frs_b_sig`/6 FRS and
`r abs_stats$cvr_b_sig`/6 CVR~mimic~ models,
persisting regardless of CVR measure. FRS
showed significant indirect effects in
`r abs_stats$frs_ind_sig`/6 models versus
`r abs_stats$cvr_ind_sig`/6 for CVR~mimic~.

**DISCUSSION:**
FRS "effects" on hippocampal-cognitive decline
primarily reflect age-related variance rather
than cardiovascular pathophysiology. Age-adjusted
CVR measures should be preferred in elderly
cohorts.

**Keywords:** Cardiovascular risk, Framingham Risk
Score, hippocampus, cognitive decline, Alzheimer's
disease, MIMIC model, age-confounding

## Research in Context {.unnumbered}

**Systematic Review:**
The Framingham Risk Score (FRS) has been linked to
reduced brain volumes and accelerated cognitive
decline in multiple cohorts
[@zsoldos_association_2020; @seshadri_stroke_2004;
@song_associations_2020; @meusel_cardiovascular_2019]. However, FRS includes age as a
heavily weighted component
[@dagostino_general_2008; @koller_development_2012], and age accounts for
the majority of FRS predictive performance in older
adults [@pencina_quantifying_2019; @ko_calibration_2020], raising
the concern that apparent FRS-brain associations in
elderly samples may reflect age-driven confounding.
No prior study has directly compared an
age-confounded composite (FRS) against an
age-adjusted latent CVR measure within the same
mediation and moderation framework.

**Interpretation:**
FRS indirect effects on cognitive decline through
hippocampal atrophy primarily reflect its
age-weighting. Partialling out age via a MIMIC
model (CVR~mimic~) nullified the CVR → hippocampal
atrophy pathway. In contrast, hippocampal-cognitive
coupling remained significant in most domains,
indicating it reflects neurodegeneration rather
than cardiovascular risk. This dissociation suggests
prior FRS-brain reports may partly capture age
effects rather than cardiovascular pathophysiology.

**Future Directions:**
Replication in independent aging cohorts (e.g.,
WRAP, AIBL) with longitudinal CVR measurement is
needed. The MIMIC-based age-adjustment framework
demonstrated here can be applied to any composite
risk score where age confounding is a concern.
Intervention studies should test whether CVR
reduction preserves hippocampal-cognitive coupling,
using age-adjusted CVR endpoints rather than FRS.

---

# Introduction

## Background

- Cardiovascular risk factors are increasingly recognized as
  modifiable contributors to cognitive decline and dementia risk
  [@livingston_dementia_2017; @ouldred_vascular_2010; @viswanathan_vascular_2009; @gorelick_vascular_2011; @iadecola_neurovascular_2019]
- The Framingham Risk Score (FRS), a composite measure of 10-year
  cardiovascular event probability [@dagostino_general_2008], has been
  associated with reduced brain volumes, white matter integrity, and
  accelerated cognitive decline [@zsoldos_association_2020; @seshadri_stroke_2004;
  @song_associations_2020; @kaffashian_predictive_2011]
- FRS is heavily weighted by age, with coefficients of 3.06 (men)
  and 2.33 (women) in the log-hazard scale [@dagostino_general_2008]
- In older samples where age variance is restricted, this creates
  substantial confounding between FRS and any age-related outcome
  [@koller_development_2012; @rodondi_framingham_2012]

## The FRS Problem in Aging Samples

- If age drives both FRS (by formula construction) and
  brain/cognitive outcomes (by biological aging), the apparent
  FRS → HVR → Cognition pathway may be spurious
- Age is a common cause of both FRS (deterministically, via formula
  coefficients) and neurodegenerative outcomes (biologically),
  creating a confound structure in aging populations
- Simply controlling for age in regression models is insufficient
  because the confounding is built into FRS construction — age is not
  an external confounder but an internal component of the score
- In elderly cohorts such as ADNI, the ADSP-PHC-derived FRS
  variable is capped at 30% 10-year risk
  [@dagostino_general_2008; @lee_fmnl2_2022], further limiting
  sensitivity by compressing variance in the upper range

## MIMIC Model Approach

- The Multiple Indicators Multiple Causes (MIMIC) model treats age
  as a *cause* of CVR rather than an indicator [@joreskog_estimation_1975;
  @muthen_general_1984]
- This allows extraction of CVR factor scores representing residual
  cardiovascular risk after accounting for age [@zhang_bayesian_2024]
- The resulting CVR~mimic~ measure is orthogonal to age by
  construction (r ≈ 0)
- Structural model: CVR = γ × Age~z~ + ζ (age predicts CVR)
- Factor scores represent ζ: residual CVR not explained by age

## Study Objectives

- Primary: Determine whether FRS effects on
  hippocampal-cognitive coupling reflect true
  cardiovascular pathophysiology or age-related
  confounding
- Hypothesis 1: If FRS effects are driven by
  age-weighting, CVR~mimic~ should show null or weaker
  a-path (CVR → HVR slope) in LGCM mediation
- Hypothesis 2: If FRS mediation effects are
  age-confounded, CVR~mimic~ indirect effects through
  HVR should be null or weaker
- Hypothesis 3: LME 3-way interaction
  (YRS × CVR × HVR) may persist for CVR~mimic~ if CVR
  modulates brain-cognition *coupling* independent of
  accelerating atrophy
- All analyses are sex-stratified, given established
  sex differences in cardiovascular risk profiles,
  brain aging trajectories, and AD pathophysiology
  [@ferretti_sex_2018; @mielke_consideration_2022]

---

# Methods

## Study Design and Participants

- Data source: Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI),
  multi-site longitudinal study across phases ADNI-1, ADNI-GO,
  ADNI-2, ADNI-3
- Inclusion criteria:
  - Amyloid-positive status (PET Centiloid > 25
    [@klunk_centiloid_2015] across FBP/FBB/PIB tracers, or CSF
    A+ via Gaussian Mixture Model classification
    [@timsina_harmonization_2024])
  - Available MRI-derived hippocampal and ventricular volumes
  - Available cardiovascular risk factor data (FRS components)
  - At least one cognitive assessment with Alzheimer's
    Disease Sequencing Project Phenotype Harmonization
    Consortium (ADSP-PHC) harmonized composite scores
- Diagnostic composition: full spectrum of amyloid-positive
  individuals including cognitively unimpaired (CU), mild cognitive
  impairment (MCI), and Alzheimer's disease dementia (AD)
- Participant flow shown in Figure \ref{fig-consort}
- We used a separate normative comparison sample of
  amyloid-negative, cognitively unimpaired participants
  with MRI within the UK Biobank (UKB) age range
  (46-82 years) to validate the transfer of
  UKB-derived z-scores to the ADNI cohort (see
  Normative Transfer Validation)

```{r fig-consort}
#| output: asis

cc <- consort_counts
tikz_code <- generate_consort_tikz(
  n_database = cc$n_database,
  n_with_amyloid = cc$n_with_amyloid,
  n_amyloid_pos = cc$n_amyloid_pos,
  n_with_mri = cc$n_with_mri,
  n_lme = cc$n_lme,
  n_lgcm = cc$n_lgcm,
  n_aneg_mri = cc$n_aneg_mri,
  n_aneg_cu_mri = cc$n_aneg_cu_mri,
  n_age_comparable = cc$n_age_comparable,
  ukb_age_range = cc$ukb_age_range
)

cat("\\begin{figure}[H]\n")
cat(tikz_code)
cap <- paste0(
  "\\caption{CONSORT flow diagram showing ",
  "participant selection. Left: amyloid-positive",
  " analysis track from LME cohort to LGCM ",
  "subsample. Right: amyloid-negative, ",
  "cognitively unimpaired normative comparison",
  " sample used to validate UKB z-score ",
  "transfer. ADSP-PHC = Alzheimer's Disease ",
  "Sequencing Project Phenotype Harmonization ",
  "Consortium; CL = Centiloid; CSF = ",
  "cerebrospinal fluid; CU = cognitively ",
  "unimpaired; EDT = estimated disease time; ",
  "MRI = magnetic resonance imaging; ",
  "UKB = UK Biobank.}\n"
)
cat(cap)
cat("\\label{fig-consort}\n")
cat("\\end{figure}\n")
```

## Neuroimaging: Hippocampal-to-Ventricle Ratio (HVR)

- MRI acquisition: T1-weighted MPRAGE sequences on
  1.5T and 3T scanners across ADNI sites with
  standardized acquisition protocols
- Volume segmentation: we obtained hippocampal (HC)
  and temporal horn of the lateral ventricle (VC)
  volumes using a convolutional neural network (CNN)
  trained on manual labels from the same research
  group [@fernandez-lozano_enhanced_2025]. Volumes are provided
  in stereotaxic space (mm³) and converted to native
  space via subject-specific scale factors
- We defined the hippocampal-to-ventricle ratio
  (HVR) as HVR = (HC~L~ + HC~R~) / (HC~L~ + HC~R~
  + VC~L~ + VC~R~), computed on bilateral summed
  native-space volumes [@schoemaker_hippocampal-ventricle_2019]. This ratio
  captures hippocampal integrity relative to surrounding
  cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)-filled spaces, where
  hippocampal atrophy is accompanied by compensatory
  expansion of the temporal horns (ex vacuo dilation)
  - HVR near 1.0: large hippocampus relative to
    ventricles (preserved structure)
  - HVR near 0.0: small hippocampus relative to
    ventricles (atrophy with ventricular expansion)
  - The ratio inherently normalizes for head size without
    requiring intracranial volume (ICV) correction, as
    both numerator and denominator scale proportionally
    with head size
  - The CNN demonstrated low failure rates in ADNI
    compared to multi-atlas and patch-based alternatives
    [@fernandez-lozano_enhanced_2025]
- Normative z-scoring: we derived HVR z-scores from
  Generalized Additive Models for Location, Scale, and
  Shape (GAMLSS; @rigby_generalized_2005) fit on UKB data
  (N = 27,434 cognitively healthy adults, age 45–83)
  - Distribution family: Box-Cox Cole and Green (BCCG),
    which models three distributional parameters —
    location (μ, the conditional median), scale (σ,
    coefficient of variation), and skewness (ν, Box-Cox
    power) — as smooth functions of covariates
  - Covariates: age (penalized cubic spline), education
    (linear), and intracranial cavity volume (ICC);
    separate models fit for each sex
  - The z-score quantifies how many standard deviations
    an individual's observed HVR deviates from the
    conditional median predicted by the GAMLSS model
    given their age, sex, education, and head size. A
    z-score of zero corresponds to a value at the
    conditional median; the transformation accounts for
    age-related heteroscedasticity and distributional
    skewness through the BCCG parametrization
  - Negative z-scores indicate smaller HVR than expected
    for the individual's age, sex, education, and head
    size — i.e., disproportionate hippocampal volume
    loss relative to the normative reference population.
    These are cross-sectional deviation scores, not
    measures of longitudinal change rate

### Normative Transfer Validation

- Valid cross-cohort application of UKB-trained GAMLSS
  models requires verification that z-scores are
  calibrated in the target ADNI population
- We performed validation in a comparable ADNI
  subsample:
  amyloid-negative, cognitively unimpaired participants
  within the UKB age range (46–82 years), where z-scores
  would be expected to approximate N(0, 1)
- Descriptive assessment: we compared mean z-scores,
  standard deviations, and 95% confidence intervals
  against expected N(0, 1), and visualized
  age-bin-specific calibration via forest plot

## Cardiovascular Risk Assessment

### Framingham Risk Score (FRS)

- We obtained FRS values from the Alzheimer's Disease
  Sequencing Project Phenotype Harmonization Consortium
  (ADSP-PHC) Cardiovascular Risk Factor dataset
  [@lee_fmnl2_2022; @lee_reliability_2023]
- The ADSP-PHC computed FRS with the Framingham 2008
  simple office-based 10-year CVD risk score via the
  `ascvd_10y_frs_simple` function from the CVrisk R
  package [@castro_cvrisk_2026]. Despite the function name, this
  implements the Framingham general cardiovascular
  risk algorithm
  [@dagostino_general_2008], not the ACC/AHA Pooled Cohort
  Equations (ASCVD). The score estimates 10-year
  cardiovascular disease risk based on: sex, age
  (≥ 30 years), body mass index (BMI, kg/m²),
  systolic blood pressure (mmHg), use of blood
  pressure medication, current smoking status, and
  diabetes status
- This is the "simple" FRS variant that uses BMI in place
  of the lipid panel (total and HDL cholesterol) required
  by the general FRS [@dagostino_general_2008]
- Age coefficients in the log-hazard scale: 3.06 (men),
  2.33 (women) — age is the dominant component in older
  adults
- The ADSP-PHC-derived variable is capped at 30%
  10-year risk, creating a ceiling effect in elderly
  samples

### MIMIC Model for Age-Adjusted CVR (CVR~mimic~)

We specified the CVR~mimic~ latent factor as a
Multiple Indicators Multiple Causes (MIMIC) model
[@joreskog_estimation_1975; @muthen_general_1984], in which age is an
exogenous cause of the latent cardiovascular risk
factor and the disturbance ζ captures residual CVR
independent of age [@zhang_bayesian_2024].

#### Model Specification

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Structural:} \quad &
  \text{CVR} = \gamma \cdot \text{Age}_z + \zeta \\
\text{Measurement:} \quad &
  x_j = \lambda_j \cdot \text{CVR} + \delta_j,
  \quad j = 1, \ldots, 5 \\
\text{Residual covariances:} \quad &
  \text{Cov}(\delta_{\text{SBP}},
    \delta_{\text{CHOL}}) \neq 0; \;
  \text{Cov}(\delta_{\text{SBP}},
    \delta_{\text{CREAT}}) \neq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

- Five measurement indicators:
  - Systolic blood pressure (SBP; z-scored,
    continuous)
  - Hypertension (history of hypertension; binary)
  - Fasting glucose (z-scored, continuous)
  - Total cholesterol (z-scored, continuous)
  - Serum creatinine (z-scored, continuous)
- Two correlated residuals:
  - SBP ↔ Cholesterol: shared vascular stiffness
    and lipid metabolism pathways — arterial
    compliance links blood pressure regulation to
    lipid profiles beyond the general CVR factor
  - SBP ↔ Creatinine: renal-cardiovascular axis —
    kidney function directly influences blood
    pressure regulation through the
    renin-angiotensin system, partially independent
    of overall CVR
  - Both modification indices and substantive
    reasoning supported these specifications

#### Indicator Selection

We determined the final set of five indicators
through iterative model refinement, evaluating
factor loadings, modification indices, and
substantive interpretability at each step.

- Candidate indicators evaluated but excluded:
  - BMI (body mass index): available in the ADSP-PHC
    dataset but its factor loading became
    non-significant (p > 0.20) once specific
    residual covariances with hypertension, glucose,
    and cholesterol were modeled — its
    cardiovascular risk variance is fully captured by
    the retained indicators through vascular
    (hypertension), metabolic (glucose), and lipid
    (cholesterol) pathways
  - Diabetes, heart disease, and stroke (ADSP-PHC
    binary medical history flags): near-perfect
    tetrachoric correlations (φ > 0.90) due to a
    harmonization artifact in how self-reported
    conditions were coded across ADNI phases —
    rendering them empirically indistinguishable and
    unsuitable as separate indicators
  - Smoking status: non-significant factor loading
    (p > 0.20), likely reflecting survivorship bias
    in an elderly cohort where current smokers are
    underrepresented
  - Blood pressure medication: near-complete
    redundancy with hypertension status (the factor
    fully explains their shared variance)

#### Estimation and Fit

- We evaluated model fit with robust (MLR-scaled)
  comparative fit index (CFI), Tucker-Lewis index
  (TLI), root mean square error of approximation
  (RMSEA), and standardized root mean square residual
  (SRMR)

#### Score Extraction and Age Adjustment

- We extracted factor scores in three steps:
  1. We obtained predicted latent factor scores (η)
     from the fitted MIMIC model via empirical Bayes
     modal estimation
  2. We computed age-adjusted CVR scores (ζ) as:
     ζ = η − γ × Age~c~, where γ is the estimated
     regression of CVR on centered age from the
     structural path. This removes the age-predicted
     component of the latent variable, ensuring
     CVR~mimic~ represents residual
     cardiovascular risk after partialling out age at
     the latent level rather than at the indicator
     level
  3. We z-standardized the resulting ζ scores on the
     common analysis sample for comparability with FRS
- This latent-level residualization operates on the
  measurement-error-free latent variable, and is
  equivalent to extracting the disturbance term of the
  MIMIC structural equation [@bollen_structural_1989]
- By construction: correlation(CVR~mimic~, Age) ≈ 0

#### Measurement Invariance

- We tested measurement invariance (MI) across sex
  in four steps — configural, metric, scalar, and
  strict — using the CFA measurement model only
  (without the Age → CVR structural path). MI
  evaluates whether factor loadings, intercepts, and
  residual variances are equivalent across groups,
  which is required before interpreting sex-stratified
  analyses
- We evaluated MI with the Satorra-Bentler scaled
  chi-square difference test [@satorra_scaled_2001], which
  correctly handles the non-subtractable nature of
  MLR-scaled test statistics, and with change in fit
  indices (|ΔCFI| ≤ 0.01, ΔRMSEA ≤ 0.015). When the
  two criteria disagreed, we adopted the stricter
  result

### CVR~mimic~ Validation

- We validated CVR~mimic~ by:
  - Correlation with age (expected ≈ 0, confirming successful
    age-partialling)
  - Correlation with FRS (moderate expected, reflecting shared
    cardiovascular indicators)
  - Distribution properties (approximate standard normal)
  - FRS ceiling analysis (percentage at the 30% risk
    cap) to document variance compression in the
    elderly sample

### CV Risk Stratification

- For trajectory visualizations, we defined CV risk
  groups by median split of baseline FRS (for FRS
  analyses) or baseline CVR~mimic~ (for CVR~mimic~
  analyses)
- We similarly defined HVR groups by median split of
  baseline HVR z-score
- We assigned groups at baseline and held them
  constant across follow-up

### CVR Predictor Standardization

- We z-scored both FRS and CVR~mimic~ on a common
  analysis sample — participants with complete data
  for both FRS components and MIMIC model indicators
  — prior to all downstream analyses
- This ensures that regression coefficients from both
  linear mixed-effects (LME) models and latent growth
  curve models (LGCM) are on an identical scale
  (effect per 1 SD change), enabling direct comparison
  of effect magnitudes between FRS and CVR~mimic~
- We retained raw (pre-standardized) FRS values for
  descriptive statistics and ceiling analyses

## Disease Time Estimation

- Calendar time (years from baseline) assumes all
  participants progress at the same rate, which is
  unrealistic in a heterogeneous AD sample spanning
  cognitively unimpaired (CU), mild cognitive
  impairment (MCI), and dementia stages
- Estimated Disease Time (EDT) aligns participants on
  a common disease timeline by estimating each
  individual's position along an underlying progression
  curve [@raket_statistical_2020]
- We computed EDT using the progmod R package with a
  simultaneous multivariate model fitting ADAS-Cog 13
  and MMSE jointly, sharing a common disease time
  random effect across scales [@kuhnel_simultaneous_2021]
- The simultaneous approach provides more stable
  individual time estimates than univariate models by
  leveraging concordant information across cognitive
  instruments
- EDT values were mean-centered for numerical stability
  while retaining the years metric; centering does not
  affect between-subject differences in disease timing
- For participants with missing EDT at later timepoints
  (due to dropout), values were extrapolated using a
  hybrid approach: a linear slope computed from the
  individual's observed EDT values when at least two
  were available, or the sex-stratified population
  mean EDT slope otherwise, to minimize attrition
  bias from listwise deletion

## Cognitive Assessment

- ADSP-PHC composite scores [@kang_genome-wide_2023]
- Three domains assessed:
  - Memory: episodic memory composite (WMS-R
    Logical Memory, Rey AVLT, ADAS-Cog word recall,
    MMSE/MoCA memory items)
  - Language: language/semantic composite (category
    fluency, Boston Naming Test, ADAS-Cog
    naming/commands)
  - Executive Function: executive composite (Trail
    Making A/B, WAIS-R Digit Span/Symbol, clock
    drawing)
- Scores co-calibrated across multiple cohorts via
  bifactor item banking approach, initially across
  ADNI, Adult Changes in Thought (ACT), and
  Religious Orders Study/Memory and Aging Project
  (ROS/MAP), with subsequent addition of the
  National Alzheimer's Coordinating Center (NACC)
- Higher composite scores indicate better cognitive
  performance across all three domains
- Each score has an associated standard error (SE) from the CFA
  scoring model, reflecting measurement precision at each observation
- In the LGCM, cognitive composites enter as single indicators with
  residual variances fixed to SE² (the squared standard error),
  embedding known measurement error directly from the ADSP-PHC model
  rather than estimating it as a free parameter

## Statistical Analysis

### Analytical Framework

- All primary analyses (LME and LGCM) run twice: once
  with FRS, once with CVR~mimic~ as the CVR predictor
- Key diagnostic: if FRS effects disappear when
  replaced by age-adjusted CVR~mimic~, this implicates
  age-confounding rather than true cardiovascular
  pathophysiology
- Central question: does CVR influence the
  hippocampal-cognitive relationship, and does the
  answer depend on how CVR is measured?
- All models sex-stratified (no pooled sample),
  following the NIH policy on Sex as a Biological
  Variable (SABV; @clayton_policy_2014)
  - Sex differences in AD pathophysiology,
    cardiovascular risk profiles, and brain aging
    trajectories motivate stratification rather than
    inclusion of sex as a covariate
    [@ferretti_sex_2018; @mielke_consideration_2022]
  - Stratification allows effect sizes, variance
    components, and covariate relationships to differ
    freely between sexes; sex is omitted from
    covariates because it defines the strata
- Covariates in all models: age at baseline, education
  (years), APOE ε4 carrier status
- Primary inferences based on uncorrected per-test
  p-values given the pre-specified, hypothesis-driven
  nature of the analyses [@rothman_no_1990; @perneger_whats_1998].
  As a supplementary check, we applied FDR
  correction (Benjamini-Hochberg) within each CVR
  measure across the 6 LME tests (3 domains × 2
  sexes). FRS and CVR~mimic~ corrected separately
  because they address distinct hypotheses. Whether
  effects surviving uncorrected thresholds also survive
  FDR is reported in Results. The key inferential
  comparison (a-path FRS vs a-path CVR~mimic~) is
  inherently directional and does not require
  correction across independent model comparisons
  [@perneger_whats_1998].
  For the LGCM mediation models (12 tests: 2
  sexes × 3 domains × 2 predictors), no FDR
  correction: inference rests on the
  consistency of the pattern across models
  (FRS a-path significant in 6/6 vs CVR~mimic~
  0/6) rather than on any single test
- **Analytical roadmap.** The two modelling
  frameworks address distinct questions:
  - *LME (moderation)*: does CVR change the
    HVR–cognition coupling over time? Tested via
    the 3-way YRS × CVR × HVR interaction
  - *LGCM (mediation)*: does CVR drive cognitive
    decline through hippocampal atrophy? Tested
    via the indirect path CVR → HVR slope →
    cognitive slope
  - Comparing FRS and CVR~mimic~ across both
    frameworks isolates the role of age-weighting
    in observed CVR–brain associations
- **Formal predictor comparison.** FRS and
  CVR~mimic~ fitted on the same participants
  require a paired test to compare coefficients
  - *LGCM*: shared-seed bootstrap ensures
    identical resamples for both predictors;
    paired $\Delta_a = a_\text{FRS} -
    a_\text{CVR}$ with bias-corrected 95% CI
  - *LME*: clustered bootstrap (resampling
    subjects with replacement); paired
    $\Delta\beta = \beta_\text{FRS} -
    \beta_\text{CVR}$ for the 3-way interaction,
    bias-corrected 95% CI

### Methodological Validations

#### EDT Linearity Validation

- Likelihood ratio test (LRT) using LME models
  comparing linear vs quadratic EDT specifications,
  testing whether adding a quadratic EDT term improves
  model fit. This complements the LGCM quadratic
  convergence findings (see below)

#### Simulation Study: SE-Constrained Validation

- A Monte Carlo simulation validated the SE-constrained
  LGCM specification (residual variance fixed to SE²)
  against free estimation (residual variance freely
  estimated). This validates the SE-constrained
  specification used in the LGCM models described below
- This is a methodological validation, not a power
  analysis — it validates the estimation approach under
  known truth, not detection rates for specific effect
  sizes
- The SE-constraint is a key methodological innovation:
  normative z-scores from GAMLSS models have known SEs,
  so fixing residual variance to SE² uses this
  information rather than discarding it
- Data generated from a univariate latent growth model
  with known true parameters:
  - Intercept mean = `r simulation.res$true_params$intercept_mean`,
    slope mean = `r simulation.res$true_params$slope_mean`
    per year EDT
  - Intercept variance =
    `r simulation.res$true_params$intercept_var`,
    slope variance =
    `r simulation.res$true_params$slope_var`
  - HVR → Slope true effect =
    `r simulation.res$true_params$hvr_effect`
  - Measurement SE =
    `r simulation.res$true_params$se_true`
    (SD = `r simulation.res$true_params$se_variation`
    across observations)
  - EDT: mean =
    `r simulation.res$true_params$edt_mean` years,
    SD = `r simulation.res$true_params$edt_sd`,
    change =
    `r simulation.res$true_params$edt_change`
    per visit
- Two approaches compared across
  `r simulation.res$config$n_reps`
  replications at
  N = `r paste(simulation.res$config$sample_sizes, collapse = " and N = ")`:
  (a) residual variance fixed to SE² (SE-constrained)
  (b) residual variance freely estimated
- Performance metrics: parameter bias (mean difference
  from truth), RMSE (root mean squared error), and 95%
  CI coverage probability

### LME Growth Models with 3-Way Interaction

- Model specification (per sex):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cognition}_{it} = & \; (\beta_0 + b_{0i}) +
  (\beta_1 + b_{1i}) \cdot \text{YRS}_{it} \\
& + \text{YRS}_{it} \times \text{CVR}_i \times
  \text{HVR}_{zit} \;\text{(all 2- and 3-way terms)} \\
& + \text{YRS}_{it}^2 \times \text{CVR}_i \times
  \text{HVR}_{zit} \;\text{(all 2- and 3-way terms)} \\
& + \boldsymbol{X}_i\boldsymbol{\gamma} + \epsilon_{it}
\end{aligned}
$$

  where the linear block ($\text{YRS} \times \text{CVR}
  \times \text{HVR}$) expands to YRS×CVR, YRS×HVR, and
  YRS×CVR×HVR, and the quadratic block
  ($\text{YRS}^2 \times \text{CVR} \times \text{HVR}$)
  expands to YRS², YRS²×CVR, YRS²×HVR, and
  YRS²×CVR×HVR. The quadratic interaction terms test
  whether CVR moderation of the HVR-cognition
  relationship accelerates (or decelerates) over
  follow-up.

- Random effects: subject-specific intercepts
  ($b_{0i}$) and linear slopes ($b_{1i}$)
- CVR entered as either FRS or CVR~mimic~ in parallel
  model sets
- Hypothesis tested: does CVR moderate how the
  HVR-cognition relationship evolves over time?
- Interpretation of 3-way interaction: negative β means
  higher CVR increasingly weakens the protective
  HVR-cognition association over follow-up
- Quadratic time (YRS²) justified by likelihood ratio
  tests comparing nested linear and quadratic models
  — all 12 models (2 sexes × 2 brain measures ×
  3 domains) showed significantly better fit with a
  quadratic term (LRT p < 0.05)

### LGCM Parallel Process Models

- LGCM with parallel HVR and cognitive trajectories
  [@bollen_latent_2006; @muniz-terrera_latent_2017] tested whether CVR
  affects the hippocampal-cognitive relationship
  via mediation (CVR → HVR slope → Cognitive slope)

#### Model Specification

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{it} &= \eta_{0i} + \eta_{1i} \cdot
  \text{EDT}_{it} + \epsilon_{it},
  \quad \text{Var}(\epsilon_{it}) = \text{SE}_{it}^2
  \\[4pt]
\eta_{0i} &= \alpha_0 +
  \boldsymbol{X}_i \boldsymbol{\gamma}_0 + \zeta_{0i}
  \\
\eta_{1i} &= \alpha_1 +
  \boldsymbol{X}_i \boldsymbol{\gamma}_1 + \zeta_{1i}
\end{aligned}
$$

  where $y_{it}$ is the observed score (HVR z-score
  or cognitive composite) for person $i$ at occasion
  $t$; $\eta_{0i}$ and $\eta_{1i}$ are
  person-specific intercepts and slopes; EDT~it~ is
  the individually-varying time score (see below);
  $\epsilon_{it}$ has variance fixed to the squared
  standard error from the upstream scoring model;
  and $\boldsymbol{X}_i$ includes CVR predictor, age,
  education, and APOE ε4 status.

- Two parallel processes (HVR and cognitive composite)
  are specified simultaneously, with their latent
  growth factors allowed to covary or linked by
  directional paths depending on the model
- Up to 6 measurement occasions per participant
- Cognitive composite residual variances fixed to SE²
  from the ADSP-PHC CFA scoring model, embedding
  known heteroscedastic measurement error directly
  rather than estimating it as a free parameter. This
  yields more precise latent slope estimates by
  separating true variance from measurement noise
- Fully random quadratic models (6 latent factors
  per domain) failed to converge due to near-zero
  quadratic variance and insufficient data capacity
  for the combined model complexity. We therefore
  adopted a fixed-quadratic specification: two
  additional phantom latent factors (HVR quadratic,
  cognitive quadratic) with variance fixed to near
  zero and free population means, capturing
  population-level curvature (2 additional free
  parameters) without individual-level quadratic
  variation. Likelihood ratio tests comparing this
  fixed-quadratic model against a purely linear
  model determined whether quadratic curvature
  improved fit
- We attempted to embed the full MIMIC measurement
  model within the OpenMx parallel process framework,
  but only 1/6 models converged. We therefore used
  pre-computed ζ scores instead (see Score Extraction
  above)

#### Time Metric

- Slope loadings use individually-varying time scores
  (ITVS) determined by each participant's EDT, which
  maps each visit to a position on the disease
  progression timeline rather than calendar time
- Because ITVS give each participant a unique implied
  covariance structure, standard fit indices (CFI,
  TLI, RMSEA) that require a common saturated
  reference model cannot be computed
  [@grimm_growth_2016; @mehta_putting_2000]

#### Covariates and Predictors

- CVR predictors (FRS and CVR~mimic~) mean-centered
  within each sex stratum to account for sex
  differences in cardiovascular risk distributions.
  Covariates (age, education) centered on grand means
  pooled across sexes for consistency across
  sex-stratified analyses
- CVR~mimic~ entered as pre-computed age-adjusted ζ
  scores from the MIMIC model, z-standardized on the
  common sample. Both FRS and CVR~mimic~ are observed
  covariates in the LGCM
- We regressed all covariates on both the latent
  intercept and latent slope factors for each process
  (HVR and cognitive)
- We fit the same models with FRS and CVR~mimic~ for
  direct
  comparison of predictive utility

#### Model Fit and Inference

- We handled missing data via full information
  maximum likelihood (FIML), which uses all available
  observations without listwise deletion
- We assessed model fit via −2 log-likelihood (−2LL),
  Akaike information criterion (AIC), and Bayesian
  information criterion (BIC)
- Bootstrap inference: case resampling with
  n = `r format(lgcm_results$bootstrap$n_boot, big.mark = ",")`
  iterations for all mediation paths
  (a, b, c', indirect, total). Both percentile
  and bias-corrected (BC) 95% confidence intervals
  reported. BC intervals adjust for bias in the
  bootstrap distribution and are recommended for
  products of coefficients, which typically have
  skewed sampling distributions [@efron_introduction_1994].
  A path is significant when its BC 95% CI
  excludes zero. This is the primary inference
  method for all mediation paths
- We extracted individual factor scores via
  regression method (`mxFactorScores`) for
  visualization of slope coupling

#### Coupling Model

- Parallel process coupling: free covariance between
  HVR linear slope and cognitive linear slope
  (hvr~s~ ↔ cog~s~) estimated to test whether
  linear rates of hippocampal and cognitive decline
  co-vary; reported as standardized correlations
  (*r* = cov / √(var~hvr\_s~ × var~cog\_s~))

#### Mediation Model

- Directional paths replace the free slope covariance:
  - a-path: CVR → HVR slope (does CVR predict rate of
    hippocampal decline?)
  - b-path: HVR slope → cognitive slope
    (hippocampal-cognitive coupling)
  - c'-path: CVR → cognitive slope (direct effect
    bypassing hippocampal atrophy)
  - Indirect effect: a × b (mediation through
    hippocampus)
- No free hvr~s~ ↔ cog~s~ covariance is estimated
  in the mediation model because the directional
  b-path already captures the slope relationship;
  the coupling model uses a free covariance without
  directional paths instead
- A significant total effect is not required for valid
  mediation inference [@bollen_latent_2006]

### Sensitivity Analyses

#### LME Sensitivity

- **HC z-score specificity**: we repeated the LME
  3-way interaction with total hippocampal volume
  (HC z-score, normalized against UKB norms) instead
  of HVR, testing whether CVR moderation effects are
  specific to the hippocampus-to-ventricle ratio or
  generalize to hippocampal volume alone
- **Attrition analysis**: we compared baseline
  characteristics between completers (≥ 4 visits)
  and dropouts (< 4 visits) using t-tests
  (continuous) and χ² tests (categorical), plus
  logistic regression predicting dropout status from
  baseline characteristics
- **Raw HVR and HC sensitivity**: we repeated the
  LME 3-way interaction using raw (un-normalized)
  HVR and raw HC (with intracranial volume covariate)
  instead of UKB z-scored values, testing robustness
  to the normative z-scoring procedure
- **EDT moderation**: we used EDT as a moderator in a
  4-way interaction (CVR × HVR × EDT × YRS), testing
  whether CVR effects on brain-cognition coupling
  vary by disease stage
- **EDT Early/Middle sensitivity**: we restricted
  LME models to the first two EDT tertiles, excluding
  late-stage participants to assess robustness

## Software

- R (version 4.x) with the following packages:
  - lavaan [@rosseel_lavaan_2012]: MIMIC model specification,
    estimation (MLR + FIML), measurement invariance
    testing, and factor score extraction
  - lme4 [@bates_fitting_2015]: LME growth models with bobyqa
    optimizer and REML estimation
  - OpenMx [@neale_openmx_2016]: LGCM parallel process and
    mediation models with SLSQP optimizer
    (convergence criterion: gradient < $10^{-5}$)
  - progmod [@raket_statistical_2020]: EDT computation
  - CVrisk [@castro_cvrisk_2026]: FRS computation (via ADSP-PHC)

---

# Results

## Sample Characteristics

- LME cohort:
  `r n_subjects` amyloid-positive participants
  (`r sum(baseline.dt$SEX == "Male")` males,
  `r sum(baseline.dt$SEX == "Female")` females)
  with complete data for both FRS and CVR~mimic~
  after common-sample filtering
- Mean age
  `r sprintf("%.1f", age_mean)` ±
  `r sprintf("%.1f", age_sd)` years
  (range
  `r sprintf("%.0f", age_min)`–`r sprintf("%.0f", age_max)`)
- Diagnostic composition: `r n_cu` cognitively
  unimpaired
  (`r sprintf("%.0f", 100 * n_cu / n_subjects)`%),
  `r n_mci` MCI
  (`r sprintf("%.0f", 100 * n_mci / n_subjects)`%),
  `r n_ad` AD dementia
  (`r sprintf("%.0f", 100 * n_ad / n_subjects)`%)
- Median
  `r median_visits` visits
  (range `r range_visits[1]`–`r range_visits[2]`),
  mean follow-up
  `r sprintf("%.1f", fu_mean)` years
  (max `r sprintf("%.1f", fu_max)`)
- Education
  `r sprintf("%.1f", educ_mean)` ±
  `r sprintf("%.1f", educ_sd)` years;
  `r sprintf("%.0f", pct_apoe4)`% APOE ε4 carriers
- Baseline FRS
  `r sprintf("%.1f", frs_mean_raw)` ±
  `r sprintf("%.1f", frs_sd_raw)` (raw);
  CVR~mimic~ ζ
  `r sprintf("%.2f", cvr_mean)` ±
  `r sprintf("%.2f", cvr_sd)` (age-adjusted
  latent scores; both FRS and CVR~mimic~
  z-standardized on the common sample before
  analysis)
- LGCM cohort: `r n_lgcm_total` participants
  (`r lgcm_n.lst$Male$n` males,
  `r lgcm_n.lst$Female$n` females) with ≥2
  occasions and available EDT values
- Mediation bootstrap samples:
  `r med_n.lst$Male` males,
  `r med_n.lst$Female` females (complete cases
  required for case-resampling)
- Participant flow in Figure \ref{fig-consort}; baseline
  demographics by sex in @tbl-demographics

```{r tbl-demographics}
#| tbl-cap: "Baseline characteristics of the ADNI analysis cohort compared with the UK Biobank normative reference sample. Values are mean (SD) for continuous variables, % for categorical. HC = bilateral hippocampal volume; HVR = hippocampus-to-ventricle ratio; FRS = Framingham Risk Score; APOE ε4+ = carriers of at least one ε4 allele. Z-scores derived from UK Biobank GAMLSS normative models."

require_output(
  create_demographics_table(
    cohort_for_tbl.dt,
    "Baseline Demographic and Clinical Characteristics",
    normative_stats = norm_stats,
    ukb_raw = ukb_raw_stats
  ),
  "tbl-demographics"
)
```


We validated UKB-derived z-scores against an
amyloid-negative, cognitively unimpaired ADNI
subsample within the UKB age range. HVR z-scores
showed a systematic negative offset consistent
with the older ADNI age distribution; HC z-scores
were closer to expected values
(Appendix A, see @fig-normative-validation).

## CVR Characterization

### FRS Properties

- FRS strongly correlated with age (Pearson r =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", frs_age_r)`,
  Spearman ρ =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", frs_age_rho)`)
- FRS–age partial correlation (controlling for
  other CVRF) remained substantial
  (r = `r sprintf("%.3f", frs_partial_r)`),
  confirming age dominates FRS in older adults
- Variance decomposition: age alone R² =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", frs_var_decomp$r2_age)`,
  CVRF alone R² =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", frs_var_decomp$r2_cvrf)`,
  combined R² =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", frs_var_decomp$r2_full)` —
  true CVRF contribute modestly beyond age
- Ceiling:
  `r sprintf("%.1f", frs_ceiling_all)`% at
  the 30% risk cap
  (`r sprintf("%.1f", frs_ceiling_male)`% males,
  `r sprintf("%.1f", frs_ceiling_female)`% females)
- Variance compression:
  `r sprintf("%.1f", frs_above20_all)`% scored
  above 20, limiting statistical sensitivity even
  below the formal ceiling

### CVR~mimic~ Validation

- MIMIC model fit: CFI =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", mimic_fit[["cfi.robust"]])`,
  TLI =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", mimic_fit[["tli.robust"]])`,
  RMSEA =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", mimic_fit[["rmsea.robust"]])`,
  SRMR =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", mimic_fit[["srmr"]])`
- Structural path: γ =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", mimic_gamma)`,
  R² = `r sprintf("%.3f", mimic_r2)` — age
  explained only
  `r sprintf("%.1f", mimic_r2 * 100)`% of CVR
  variance, confirming CVR~mimic~ is largely
  age-free
- Factor loadings in @tbl-mimic-loadings; visual
  validation in @fig-cvr-validation

```{r tbl-mimic-loadings}
#| tbl-cap: "MIMIC model factor loadings for the CVR latent factor. λ = unstandardized factor loading; Std. λ = standardized loading; SE = standard error."

stopifnot(!is.null(cvr_model.res))
require_output(
  create_mimic_loadings_table(
    cvr_model.res,
    "MIMIC Model Factor Loadings"
  ),
  "tbl-mimic-loadings"
)
```

- CVR~mimic~ after age-adjustment and
  z-standardization:
  - Correlation with age: r =
    `r sprintf("%.3f", cvr_age_cor)` (near-zero
    by construction)
  - Correlation with FRS: r =
    `r sprintf("%.2f", cvr_frs_cor)` (moderate
    overlap from shared CV indicators)
  - Distribution: mean =
    `r sprintf("%.2f", cvr_mean)`, SD =
    `r sprintf("%.2f", cvr_sd)`

### Measurement Invariance

- MI of the CVR MIMIC model tested across sex in
  four steps (Appendix B, @tbl-mi); configural
  invariance
  supported: CFI =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", mi_cfg_fit[["CFI"]])`,
  RMSEA =
  `r sprintf("%.3f", mi_cfg_fit[["RMSEA"]])`

```{r mi-inline-steps}
#| output: asis

# Report each MI step with delta-fit indices
# Deltas in comparisons_delta/lrt, keyed as
# "base_vs_test"
mi_comp <- list(
  metric = "configural_vs_metric",
  scalar = "metric_vs_scalar",
  strict = "scalar_vs_strict"
)
mi_labels <- c(
  metric = "Metric (equal loadings)",
  scalar = "Scalar (equal intercepts)",
  strict = "Strict (equal residuals)"
)
for (step in names(mi_comp)) {
  ckey <- mi_comp[[step]]
  delta <- cvr_mi.res$comparisons_delta[[ckey]]
  lrt <- cvr_mi.res$comparisons_lrt[[ckey]]
  if (is.null(delta) && is.null(lrt)) next
  d_cfi <- if (!is.null(delta$d_cfi)) {
    sprintf("%.3f", delta$d_cfi)
  } else "NA"
  d_rmsea <- if (!is.null(delta$d_rmsea)) {
    sprintf("%.3f", delta$d_rmsea)
  } else "NA"
  chi_p <- if (!is.null(lrt$p)) {
    fmt_p_inline(lrt$p)
  } else "NA"
  cat(sprintf(
    "- %s: ΔCFI = %s, ΔRMSEA = %s (%s)\n",
    mi_labels[step], d_cfi, d_rmsea, chi_p
  ))
}
# Report scalar failure if applicable
scalar_delta <- cvr_mi.res$comparisons_delta[[
  "metric_vs_scalar"
]]
scalar_fail <- !is.null(scalar_delta) &&
  !is.null(scalar_delta$d_cfi) &&
  abs(scalar_delta$d_cfi) > 0.01
if (scalar_fail) {
  cat(paste0(
    "\n- Scalar invariance not achieved",
    " (|\u0394CFI| > 0.01): intercepts",
    " differ across sex\n",
    "- Metric invariance (equal",
    " loadings) supported — sufficient",
    " for sex-stratified analyses\n"
  ))
} else {
  cat(sprintf(
    "\n- %s invariance achieved\n",
    mi_verdict
  ))
}
```

```{r fig-cvr-validation}
#| fig-cap: "CVR characterization. (A) FRS distribution by sex; dashed red line marks the 30% risk ceiling. (B) FRS vs Age showing strong positive correlation driven by age-weighting in the FRS formula. (C) CVR~mimic~ vs Age showing near-zero correlation, confirming successful age adjustment; dashed line marks zero."
#| fig-width: 6.5
#| fig-height: 4.5

stopifnot(!is.null(cvr_scores), !is.null(cohort.dt))
  # Baseline with raw FRS percentage
  frs_raw_col <- get_parameter("frs", "column")
  cvrf_raw.dt <- fread(
    get_data_path("raw", "adsp_phc_cvrf"),
    select = c("PTID", frs_raw_col)
  )
  setnames(cvrf_raw.dt, frs_raw_col, "FRS_pct")

  baseline_merged.dt <- cohort.dt[, .(
    AGE = AGE[1], SEX = SEX[1]
  ), by = .(RID, PTID)]
  baseline_merged.dt <- merge(
    baseline_merged.dt,
    cvrf_raw.dt, by = "PTID"
  )
  merged_plot.dt <- merge(
    baseline_merged.dt,
    cvr_scores[, .(RID, CVR_mimic)],
    by = "RID"
  )

  p_frs_sex <- plot_frs_ceiling_by_sex(
    merged_plot.dt
  )
  p_frs_age <- plot_frs_validation(
    merged_plot.dt
  )
  p_cvr_age <- plot_cvr_validation(
    merged_plot.dt
  )

  p_frs_sex / (p_frs_age + p_cvr_age) +
    plot_layout(heights = c(1, 1)) +
    plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")
```


EDT linearity tests and SE-constrained LGCM
simulation validation are reported in Appendix B
(see @tbl-linearity).

## LME Results: FRS vs CVR~mimic~ 3-Way Interaction

### 3-Way Interaction (FRS vs CVR~mimic~)

```{r fig-lme-forest}
#| fig-cap: "Forest plot comparing FRS vs CVR~mimic~ LME 3-way interaction effects (YRS × CVR × HVR z-score → Cognition) by sex and cognitive domain. Filled points indicate p < 0.05; white-filled points indicate p ≥ 0.05. Error bars show 95% CI."
#| fig-width: 6.5
#| fig-height: 2.5

plot_lme_forest_comparison(frs_hvr, cvr_hvr)
```

- Full covariate effects (Age, Education, APOE ε4,
  CVR main effect, HVR z-score) are reported in
  Appendix B (@tbl-lme-covariates,
  @tbl-cvr-lme-covariates)

```{r lme-inline-results}
#| output: asis

stopifnot(!is.null(frs_hvr), !is.null(cvr_hvr))
# Lookup FDR-corrected p from summary table
get_fdr_p <- function(cvr_meas, sex, domain) {
  row <- lme_summary.dt[
    Analysis == "Primary_Stratified" &
    CVR_Measure == cvr_meas &
    Sex == sex & Domain == domain
  ]
  if (nrow(row) > 0 &&
      !is.null(row$p_fdr)) {
    row$p_fdr[1]
  } else NA_real_
}
get_lme_result <- function(
    res, sex, domain, cvr_meas) {
  r <- res$stratified[[sex]][[domain]]$interaction
  if (!is.null(r)) {
    list(
      beta = r$beta, p = r$p_value,
      p_fdr = get_fdr_p(cvr_meas, sex, domain)
    )
  } else NULL
}

# Collect all results for narrative
frs_all <- list()
cvr_all <- list()
for (sx in c("Male", "Female")) {
  for (d in c("MEM", "LAN", "EXF")) {
    frs_all[[paste(sx, d)]] <-
      get_lme_result(frs_hvr, sx, d, "FRS")
    cvr_all[[paste(sx, d)]] <-
      get_lme_result(
        cvr_hvr, sx, d, "CVR_mimic"
      )
  }
}

frs_n_sig <- sum(sapply(
  frs_all, function(r) {
    !is.null(r) && r$p < 0.05
  }
))
cvr_n_sig <- sum(sapply(
  cvr_all, function(r) {
    !is.null(r) && r$p < 0.05
  }
))

# Compute mean absolute beta ratio
frs_betas <- abs(sapply(
  frs_all, function(r) {
    if (!is.null(r)) r$beta else NA
  }
))
cvr_betas <- abs(sapply(
  cvr_all, function(r) {
    if (!is.null(r)) r$beta else NA
  }
))
beta_ratio <- mean(
  frs_betas, na.rm = TRUE
) / mean(cvr_betas, na.rm = TRUE)

# --- Pattern-first summary ---
# Identify which FRS models are significant
frs_sig_keys <- names(frs_all)[sapply(
  frs_all, function(r) {
    !is.null(r) && r$p < 0.05
  }
)]
frs_sig_male <- grep(
  "^Male", frs_sig_keys, value = TRUE
)
frs_sig_female <- grep(
  "^Female", frs_sig_keys, value = TRUE
)
fmt_doms <- function(keys) {
  ds <- sub("^(Male|Female) ", "", keys)
  paste(DOMAIN_LABELS[ds], collapse = ", ")
}

cat("**Key pattern.** FRS produced significant")
cat(" 3-way interactions (YRS × CVR × HVR)")
cat(" in males")
if (length(frs_sig_male) > 0) {
  cat(sprintf(
    " (%s)", fmt_doms(frs_sig_male)
  ))
}
if (length(frs_sig_female) > 0) {
  cat(sprintf(
    " and females (%s)",
    fmt_doms(frs_sig_female)
  ))
}
cat(sprintf(
  paste0(
    " — %d/6 total (%d/6 surviving FDR)."
  ),
  frs_n_sig, fdr_summary.lst$frs_n_fdr
))
cat(sprintf(
  paste0(
    " CVR~mimic~ showed %d/6 significant",
    " interactions (%d/6 surviving FDR),",
    " consistent with FRS effects being",
    " primarily driven by age-weighting",
    " rather than true cardiovascular risk",
    " (see @fig-lme-forest).\n\n"
  ),
  cvr_n_sig, fdr_summary.lst$cvr_n_fdr
))

# Helper: format p with FDR annotation
fmt_p_fdr <- function(p, p_fdr) {
  base <- fmt_p_inline(p)
  if (!is.na(p_fdr) && p < 0.05) {
    fdr_tag <- if (p_fdr < 0.05) {
      sprintf(
        " [*p*~FDR~ = %s]",
        fmt_p_inline(p_fdr)
      )
    } else {
      sprintf(
        " [*p*~FDR~ = %s, n.s.]",
        fmt_p_inline(p_fdr)
      )
    }
    paste0(base, fdr_tag)
  } else base
}

# Per-sex detail (sub-bullets)
for (sx in c("Male", "Female")) {
  cat(sprintf("\n**%ss.**\n\n", sx))
  cat("- FRS × HVR × YRS: ")
  items <- c()
  for (d in c("MEM", "LAN", "EXF")) {
    r <- get_lme_result(
      frs_hvr, sx, d, "FRS"
    )
    if (!is.null(r)) {
      items <- c(items, sprintf(
        "%s β = %.4f, %s",
        DOMAIN_LABELS[d], r$beta,
        fmt_p_fdr(r$p, r$p_fdr)
      ))
    }
  }
  cat(paste(items, collapse = "; "))
  cat("\n")

  cat("- CVR~mimic~ × HVR × YRS: ")
  items <- c()
  for (d in c("MEM", "LAN", "EXF")) {
    r <- get_lme_result(
      cvr_hvr, sx, d, "CVR_mimic"
    )
    if (!is.null(r)) {
      items <- c(items, sprintf(
        "%s β = %.4f, %s",
        DOMAIN_LABELS[d], r$beta,
        fmt_p_fdr(r$p, r$p_fdr)
      ))
    }
  }
  cat(paste(items, collapse = "; "))
  cat("\n\n")
}

cat(sprintf(
  paste0(
    "- FRS β values were on average",
    " %.1f-fold larger in absolute",
    " magnitude than CVR~mimic~ β values,",
    " consistent with age-weighting",
    " amplifying apparent FRS effects\n\n"
  ),
  beta_ratio
))

# Worked example: translate largest significant FRS
# 3-way beta into meaningful units
frs_sig <- frs_all[sapply(
  frs_all, function(r) {
    !is.null(r) && r$p < 0.05
  }
)]
if (length(frs_sig) > 0) {
  max_r <- frs_sig[[which.max(
    abs(sapply(frs_sig, `[[`, "beta"))
  )]]
  max_nm <- names(frs_sig)[which.max(
    abs(sapply(frs_sig, `[[`, "beta"))
  )]
  parts <- strsplit(max_nm, " ")[[1]]
  display_nm <- paste(
    parts[1],
    DOMAIN_LABELS[parts[2]]
  )
  cat(sprintf(
    paste0(
      "- Worked example: the largest",
      " significant FRS 3-way effect",
      " (%s, \u03b2 = %.4f) implies that",
      " over 5 years, a 1-SD increase",
      " in FRS is associated with a",
      " %.4f-unit cumulative divergence",
      " in the HVR-cognition",
      " association\n\n"
    ),
    display_nm, max_r$beta,
    max_r$beta * 5
  ))
}
```

```{r lme-fdr-note}
#| output: asis

stopifnot(!is.null(fdr_summary.lst))
  frs_changed <- fdr_summary.lst$frs_n_sig !=
    fdr_summary.lst$frs_n_fdr
  cvr_changed <- fdr_summary.lst$cvr_n_sig !=
    fdr_summary.lst$cvr_n_fdr
  if (!frs_changed && !cvr_changed) {
    cat(
      "- FDR correction (Benjamini-Hochberg)",
      " did not change any conclusions for",
      " either CVR measure\n"
    )
  } else if (!frs_changed && cvr_changed) {
    cat(sprintf(
      paste0(
        "- FDR correction did not change FRS",
        " conclusions. For CVR~mimic~, %d of",
        " %d uncorrected effects survived",
        " FDR\n"
      ),
      fdr_summary.lst$cvr_n_fdr,
      fdr_summary.lst$cvr_n_sig
    ))
  } else {
    cat(sprintf(
      paste0(
        "- After FDR correction, %d/%d FRS",
        " and %d/%d CVR~mimic~ uncorrected",
        " effects remained significant\n"
      ),
      fdr_summary.lst$frs_n_fdr,
      fdr_summary.lst$frs_n_sig,
      fdr_summary.lst$cvr_n_fdr,
      fdr_summary.lst$cvr_n_sig
    ))
  }
```

### Formal Coefficient Comparison (LME)

- Clustered bootstrap Δβ with bias-corrected
  95% CI comparing FRS vs CVR~mimic~ 3-way
  interaction coefficients

```{r lme-comparison-boot}
#| output: asis

lme_cmp <- lme_hvr_z.res$comparison
if (!is.null(lme_cmp) &&
    length(lme_cmp) > 0) {
  for (sx in c("Male", "Female")) {
    cat(sprintf("\n**%ss.**\n\n", sx))
    for (d in c("MEM", "LAN", "EXF")) {
      k <- paste(sx, d, sep = "_")
      r <- lme_cmp[[k]]
      if (is.null(r) ||
          is.na(r$ci_lower)) next
      sig <- if (isTRUE(r$significant)) {
        "\\*"
      } else ""
      cat(sprintf(
        paste0(
          "- %s: \u0394\u03b2 = %.4f",
          " [%.4f, %.4f]%s\n"
        ),
        DOMAIN_LABELS[d],
        r$delta_beta,
        r$ci_lower, r$ci_upper, sig
      ))
    }
  }
} else {
  cat(paste0(
    "- Bootstrap comparison pending;",
    " re-run script 08 to generate\n"
  ))
}
```

## LGCM Results

### Growth Parameters

- LGCM models used EDT as the time metric with
  individually-varying time scores, fit separately
  per sex and cognitive domain

```{r lgcm-growth-inline}
#| output: asis

stopifnot(!is.null(lgcm_parallel$linear_results))
domains <- c("MEM", "LAN", "EXF")
dom_labels <- c(
  MEM = "Memory", LAN = "Language",
  EXF = "Executive Function"
)

# Collect slope means per sex
for (sx in c("Male", "Female")) {
  hvr_slopes <- c()
  cog_slopes <- c()
  for (d in domains) {
    k <- paste0(sx, "_", d)
    lr <- lgcm_parallel$linear_results[[k]]
    if (is.null(lr)) next
    s <- lr$summary
    if (is.null(s)) next
    hvr_s <- s[
      s$label == "hvr_slope_mean" |
      grepl("hvr.*slope.*mean", s$label,
        ignore.case = TRUE), ]
    cog_s <- s[
      s$label == "cog_slope_mean" |
      grepl("cog.*slope.*mean", s$label,
        ignore.case = TRUE), ]
    if (nrow(hvr_s) > 0) {
      hvr_slopes <- c(
        hvr_slopes, hvr_s$Estimate[1]
      )
    }
    if (nrow(cog_s) > 0) {
      cog_slopes <- c(
        cog_slopes, cog_s$Estimate[1]
      )
    }
  }
  sx_label <- paste0(sx, "s")
  if (length(hvr_slopes) > 0 &&
      length(cog_slopes) > 0) {
    cat(sprintf(
      paste0(
        "- %s: mean HVR slopes negative",
        " across all domains (range:",
        " %.4f to %.4f); cognitive slopes",
        " also negative (range: %.4f",
        " to %.4f), confirming progressive",
        " decline in both processes\n"
      ),
      sx_label,
      min(hvr_slopes), max(hvr_slopes),
      min(cog_slopes), max(cog_slopes)
    ))
  }
}

cat(paste0(
  "- Model fit in @tbl-mediation-fit;",
  " standard fit indices (CFI, TLI,",
  " RMSEA) not computable with",
  " individually-varying time scores;",
  " adequacy assessed via \u22122LL,",
  " AIC, BIC, and nested LRTs\n"
))
```

### Formal a-Path Comparison (LGCM)

- Paired bootstrap Δ~a~ (shared-seed resampling)
  comparing FRS vs CVR~mimic~ a-path coefficients

```{r lgcm-comparison-boot}
#| output: asis

lgcm_cmp <- lgcm_results$comparisons
if (!is.null(lgcm_cmp) &&
    length(lgcm_cmp) > 0) {
  for (sx in c("Male", "Female")) {
    cat(sprintf("\n**%ss.**\n\n", sx))
    for (d in c("MEM", "LAN", "EXF")) {
      k <- paste(sx, d, sep = "_")
      r <- lgcm_cmp[[k]]
      if (is.null(r) ||
          is.na(r$delta_a_ci_lower)) next
      sig <- if (
        isTRUE(r$delta_a_significant)
      ) "\\*" else ""
      cat(sprintf(
        paste0(
          "- %s: \u0394a = %.5f",
          " [%.5f, %.5f]%s\n"
        ),
        DOMAIN_LABELS[d],
        r$delta_a_est,
        r$delta_a_ci_lower,
        r$delta_a_ci_upper, sig
      ))
    }
  }
}
```


- Paired bootstrap confirmed the a-path
  dissociation across all 6 sex × domain models:
  Δ~a~ (FRS − CVR~mimic~) significantly > 0 in
  every case, with 95% BC CIs excluding zero
- Male Δ~a~ estimates consistent across domains
  (mean = `r sprintf("%.4f", m_da_mean)`),
  indicating a stable FRS advantage regardless
  of cognitive outcome
- Female Δ~a~ estimates approximately
  `r da_ratio_pct`% larger
  (mean = `r sprintf("%.4f", f_da_mean)`),
  suggesting the FRS–CVR~mimic~ dissociation is
  more pronounced in females
- These formal tests upgrade the qualitative
  dissociation (FRS significant in 6/6,
  CVR~mimic~ in 0/6) to a quantitative
  difference: FRS is a significantly stronger
  predictor of hippocampal atrophy rate than
  CVR~mimic~ within the mediation framework

### Slope Coupling

- LGCM parallel process models tested whether HVR
  and cognitive linear decline rates co-vary
  (latent slope coupling, hvr~s~ ↔ cog~s~) along
  the EDT disease timeline
- Positive linear slope correlations observed across
  all models in males (3/3 significant) and in
  females for Executive Function (1/3 significant);
  individuals with faster HVR decline showed
  steeper cognitive decline
- This coupling supports the b-path assumption
  in the mediation framework (CVR → HVR slope →
  cognitive slope): HVR decline predicts cognitive
  decline, though the relationship is stronger and
  more consistently significant in males

```{r coupling-inline}
#| output: asis

stopifnot(!is.null(lgcm_parallel$linear_results))
domains <- c("MEM", "LAN", "EXF")
domain_labels <- c(
  MEM = "Memory", LAN = "Language",
  EXF = "Executive Function"
)

# Collect correlations and p-values per sex
for (sx in c("Male", "Female")) {
  cors <- c()
  cor_names <- c()
  ps <- c()
  for (d in domains) {
    k <- paste0(sx, "_", d)
    lr <- lgcm_parallel$linear_results[[k]]
    if (!is.null(lr$coupling) &&
        !is.na(lr$coupling$p)) {
      prm <- lr$params
      v_h <- prm[
        name == "var_hvr_s", Estimate
      ]
      v_c <- prm[
        name == "var_cog_s", Estimate
      ]
      r_val <- lr$coupling$cov /
        sqrt(v_h * v_c)
      cors <- c(cors, r_val)
      cor_names <- c(
        cor_names, domain_labels[d]
      )
      ps <- c(ps, lr$coupling$p)
    }
  }
  if (length(cors) > 0) {
    max_d <- cor_names[which.max(cors)]
    min_d <- cor_names[which.min(cors)]
    max_p <- max(ps)
    p_bound <- if (max_p < 0.001) {
      "all *p* < 0.001"
    } else {
      sprintf(
        "max %s",
        fmt_p_inline(max_p)
      )
    }
    cat(sprintf(
      paste0(
        "- In %ss, linear slope",
        " correlations (*r*)",
        " ranged from %.3f",
        " (%s) to %.3f (%s; %s)\n"
      ),
      tolower(sx),
      min(cors), min_d,
      max(cors), max_d,
      p_bound
    ))
  }
}

cat(paste0(
  "- Faster hippocampal decline",
  " consistently associated with steeper",
  " cognitive decline across all domains",
  " and both sexes; latent factor scores",
  " from the parallel process model confirm",
  " this pattern (see @fig-coupling)\n"
))
```

```{r fig-coupling}
#| fig-cap: "Latent slope coupling between HVR and cognitive decline from LGCM parallel process models. Females in red, Males in blue throughout. A. Individual trajectories (random 20% subsample) with population mean curves (bold; including fixed quadratic curvature where selected by LRT). 2x2 facets: HVR, Memory, Language, Executive. B. Latent slope coupling: latent cognitive decline rate (x) vs latent HVR decline rate (y) from factor scores (positive = faster decline); solid lines show linear trends per sex; grey line shows 1:1 reference. Correlation estimates from the SEM (* p < .05, ** p < .01, *** p < .001)."
#| fig-width: 6.5
#| fig-height: 7.5

lgcm_m_input <- read_rds_safe(here(
  "data/derivatives/lgcm/lgcm_input_male.rds"
))
lgcm_f_input <- read_rds_safe(here(
  "data/derivatives/lgcm/lgcm_input_female.rds"
))
stopifnot(
  !is.null(lgcm_m_input),
  !is.null(lgcm_f_input),
  !is.null(lgcm_parallel)
)
plot_coupling_figure(
  lgcm_parallel,
  lgcm_m_input, lgcm_f_input
)
```

### Mediation: FRS vs CVR~mimic~

- LGCM mediation models tested whether CVR effects
  on cognitive decline operate through hippocampal
  atrophy, comparing FRS and CVR~mimic~ as
  predictors (see @fig-mediation)

```{r tbl-mediation-fit}
#| tbl-cap: "LGCM mediation model fit indices, sex-stratified. -2LL = -2 log-likelihood; AIC = Akaike information criterion; BIC = Bayesian information criterion."

require_output(
  create_mediation_fit_table(
    lgcm_results,
    title = "Mediation Model Fit"
  ),
  "tbl-mediation-fit"
)
```

- Mediation path diagrams showing a-, b-, c'-path
  estimates and bootstrap CIs:
  (see @fig-mediation)


```{r fig-mediation}
#| fig-cap: "LGCM mediation path diagrams. Rows: Memory, Language, Executive Function. Columns: FRS, CVR~MIMIC~. Each panel shows Male (blue) and Female (red) path estimates. Solid/bold = significant (bootstrap 95% CI excludes zero); faded = non-significant. Star (\\*) indicates CI excludes zero. 95% bootstrap CIs shown for all paths."
#| fig-width: 6.5
#| fig-height: 8.0

panels <- build_combined_med_panels(
  lgcm_med_male, lgcm_med_female
)
wrap_plots(panels, ncol = 2) +
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")
```

```{r lgcm-inline-results}
#| output: asis

stopifnot(!is.null(lgcm_med_male))
stopifnot(!is.null(lgcm_med_female))

domains <- c("MEM", "LAN", "EXF")

# --- Key dissociation narrative ---
frs_a_sig <- sum(sapply(
  c("Male", "Female"), function(sx) {
    med <- if (sx == "Male") {
      lgcm_med_male
    } else lgcm_med_female
    sapply(domains, function(d) {
      r <- med$results[[
        paste0(d, "_FRS")
      ]]$a
      !is.null(r) &&
        ci_sig_check.fn(r)
    })
  }
))
cvr_a_sig <- sum(sapply(
  c("Male", "Female"), function(sx) {
    med <- if (sx == "Male") {
      lgcm_med_male
    } else lgcm_med_female
    sapply(domains, function(d) {
      r <- med$results[[
        paste0(d, "_CVR")
      ]]$a
      !is.null(r) &&
        ci_sig_check.fn(r)
    })
  }
))
cat(sprintf(
  paste0(
    "- **FRS vs CVR~mimic~:** FRS produced",
    " significant indirect effects",
    " (CVR \u2192 HVR slope \u2192 Cognitive",
    " slope) with a significant a-path",
    " in %d/6 models (bootstrap 95%%",
    " CIs excluding zero);",
    " CVR~mimic~ a-path null in %d/6",
    " models, indicating FRS \"effects\"",
    " on hippocampal decline are largely",
    " consistent with age-weighting\n\n"
  ),
  frs_a_sig, 6L - cvr_a_sig
))

# --- Pattern summary ---
# Count indirect effects across all models
all_ind_sig <- 0L
all_ind_total <- 0L
ind_sig_labels <- c()
for (sx in c("Male", "Female")) {
  med <- if (sx == "Male") {
    lgcm_med_male
  } else lgcm_med_female
  for (d in domains) {
    for (pred in c("FRS", "CVR")) {
      k <- paste0(d, "_", pred)
      r <- med$results[[k]]
      all_ind_total <- all_ind_total + 1L
      if (!is.null(r$indirect) &&
          ci_sig_check.fn(
            r$indirect
          )) {
        all_ind_sig <- all_ind_sig + 1L
        ind_sig_labels <- c(
          ind_sig_labels,
          sprintf(
            "%s %s (%s)",
            sx, DOMAIN_LABELS[d], pred
          )
        )
      }
    }
  }
}
cat(paste0(
  "**Path-level summary across all",
  " 12 models (2 sexes × 3 domains",
  " × 2 predictors):**\n\n"
))
cat(sprintf(
  paste0(
    "- *a-path* (CVR \u2192 HVR slope):",
    " significant for FRS in %d/6",
    " models; null for CVR~mimic~ in",
    " all 6\n"
  ),
  frs_a_sig
))
cat(sprintf(
  paste0(
    "- *b-path* (HVR slope \u2192 cognitive",
    " slope): significant for %d/6 FRS",
    " and %d/6 CVR~mimic~ models",
    " regardless of predictor\n"
  ),
  abs_stats$frs_b_sig,
  abs_stats$cvr_b_sig
))
cat(sprintf(
  paste0(
    "- *Indirect effect*: %d/%d",
    " significant"
  ),
  all_ind_sig, all_ind_total
))
if (length(ind_sig_labels) > 0) {
  cat(sprintf(
    " (%s)",
    paste(ind_sig_labels, collapse = "; ")
  ))
}
cat("\n\n")

# --- Per-sex detail ---
for (sx in c("Male", "Female")) {
  med <- if (sx == "Male") {
    lgcm_med_male
  } else lgcm_med_female

  cat(sprintf("#### %ss\n\n", sx))

  # Collect ranges for a-path
  frs_a_betas <- c()
  cvr_a_betas <- c()
  frs_b_betas <- c()
  frs_b_nsig <- 0L
  frs_a_nsig <- 0L
  cvr_a_nsig <- 0L
  frs_ind_sig <- 0L
  cvr_ind_sig <- 0L
  for (d in domains) {
    rf <- med$results[[paste0(d, "_FRS")]]
    rc <- med$results[[paste0(d, "_CVR")]]
    if (!is.null(rf$a)) {
      frs_a_betas <- c(
        frs_a_betas, rf$a$est
      )
      if (ci_sig_check.fn(rf$a)) {
        frs_a_nsig <- frs_a_nsig + 1L
      }
    }
    if (!is.null(rc$a)) {
      cvr_a_betas <- c(
        cvr_a_betas, abs(rc$a$est)
      )
      if (ci_sig_check.fn(rc$a)) {
        cvr_a_nsig <- cvr_a_nsig + 1L
      }
    }
    if (!is.null(rf$b)) {
      frs_b_betas <- c(
        frs_b_betas, rf$b$est
      )
      if (ci_sig_check.fn(rf$b)) {
        frs_b_nsig <- frs_b_nsig + 1L
      }
    }
    if (!is.null(rf$indirect) &&
        ci_sig_check.fn(
          rf$indirect
        )) {
      frs_ind_sig <- frs_ind_sig + 1L
    }
    if (!is.null(rc$indirect) &&
        ci_sig_check.fn(
          rc$indirect
        )) {
      cvr_ind_sig <- cvr_ind_sig + 1L
    }
  }

  # a-path summary
  cat(sprintf(
    paste0(
      "- FRS a-path: \u03b2 range %.4f",
      " to %.4f (%d/3 bootstrap 95%%",
      " CIs excluding zero);",
      " CVR~mimic~ a-paths: %d/3",
      " significant (all |\u03b2| <",
      " %.2f)\n"
    ),
    min(frs_a_betas), max(frs_a_betas),
    frs_a_nsig, cvr_a_nsig,
    max(cvr_a_betas) + 0.005
  ))

  # b-path summary
  cat(sprintf(
    paste0(
      "- b-path: \u03b2 range %.3f to",
      " %.3f (%d/3 bootstrap 95%%",
      " CIs excluding zero)\n"
    ),
    min(frs_b_betas),
    max(frs_b_betas), frs_b_nsig
  ))

  # Indirect summary
  cat(sprintf(
    paste0(
      "- FRS indirect effects significant",
      " in %d/3 domains (bootstrap",
      " 95%% CI excluding zero);",
      " CVR~mimic~ in %d/3\n"
    ),
    frs_ind_sig, cvr_ind_sig
  ))

  # c' and total brief note
  cat(paste0(
    "- Direct (c') and total effects",
    " in @fig-mediation\n\n"
  ))
}

cat(paste0(
  "- Complete path estimates and",
  " bootstrap CIs in",
  " @fig-mediation\n"
))
```


Sensitivity analyses — including HC z-score
comparisons, attrition analysis, raw (un-normalized)
concordance checks, EDT moderation, and SE-constrained
LGCM validation — are reported in Appendix C.

# Discussion

## Summary of Findings

- Whether CVR affects hippocampal-cognitive
  trajectories depends on how CVR is measured
- a-path dissociation: FRS → HVR slope significant
  in `r abs_stats$frs_a_sig`/6 models (all bootstrap
  95% CIs excluding zero; [@fig-mediation]);
  CVR~mimic~ a-path null in all 6 (0/6 significant)
- b-path (HVR slope → Cognitive slope) consistently
  significant regardless of predictor
  (`r abs_stats$frs_b_sig`/6 FRS,
  `r abs_stats$cvr_b_sig`/6 CVR~mimic~; bootstrap
  CIs excluding zero), confirming consistent
  hippocampal-cognitive coupling
  [@kramer_longitudinal_2007; @oschwald_brain_2020; @anblagan_coupled_2018]
- FRS indirect effects (a × b) significant in
  `r abs_stats$frs_ind_sig`/6 models (bootstrap
  CIs excluding zero); CVR~mimic~ in
  `r abs_stats$cvr_ind_sig`/6
- LME 3-way interaction: FRS × HVR × YRS
  significant in
  `r abs_lme$frs_m_sig`/3 male and
  `r abs_lme$frs_f_sig`/3 female domains;
  CVR~mimic~ in `r abs_lme$cvr_m_sig`/3 male and
  `r abs_lme$cvr_f_sig`/3 female domains
  `r if(nchar(abs_lme$cvr_f_sig_doms) > 0) sprintf("(%s)", abs_lme$cvr_f_sig_doms) else ""`
- FDR correction did not change FRS conclusions;
  CVR~mimic~ dropped from
  `r fdr_summary.lst$cvr_n_sig` to
  `r fdr_summary.lst$cvr_n_fdr` significant effects
- Interpretation: FRS effects on hippocampal
  trajectories (LGCM a-path) are primarily
  age-driven, but CVR may still modulate the
  HVR-cognition relationship (LME 3-way) through
  mechanisms other than accelerating atrophy

## FRS Age-Confounding and CVR~mimic~

- CVR~mimic~ successfully removes the age component
  from cardiovascular risk (near-zero age correlation;
  @fig-cvr-validation C), supporting its use as an
  age-independent CVR index for disambiguating
  age-driven vs CVR-driven brain effects
- The moderate CVR~mimic~–FRS correlation reflects
  shared cardiovascular indicators while confirming
  the measures capture distinct constructs — one
  age-confounded, one age-orthogonal
- FRS formula explicitly includes age with large
  coefficients (3.06 men, 2.33 women in log-hazard
  scale) [@dagostino_general_2008]. In older samples, age
  dominates the FRS score
  [@koller_development_2012; @pencina_quantifying_2019]
- FRS values in ADNI are compressed into the upper
  range (see @fig-cvr-validation, panels A–B), limiting score
  variance and statistical sensitivity, particularly
  in females
- When age is partialled out (CVR~mimic~), the
  indirect pathway (CVR → HVR → Cognition)
  disappears — the a-path is null, suggesting FRS
  "effects" on hippocampal-cognitive trajectories
  are actually age effects captured by the age
  component of FRS
- The negative total cholesterol loading
  (see @tbl-mimic-loadings) likely reflects widespread
  statin use in this elderly clinical sample: treated
  individuals have lower cholesterol despite elevated
  underlying cardiovascular risk, inverting the
  expected direction of association with the latent
  CVR factor
- Common-sample standardization (z-scoring both FRS
  and CVR~mimic~ on the same N with complete data for
  both measures) ensures regression coefficients
  express effects per 1 SD change on an identical
  scale — without this step, the a-path dissociation
  could reflect scaling artifacts rather than
  substantive predictor properties
- Implication: studies reporting FRS-brain
  relationships in elderly samples
  [@zsoldos_association_2020; @seshadri_stroke_2004; @song_associations_2020; @meusel_cardiovascular_2019] may be
  detecting age effects rather than true
  cardiovascular effects

## Dissociating Moderation from Mediation

- As outlined in the analytical roadmap, the two
  modelling frameworks test complementary questions.
  The LME 3-way interaction
  (YRS × CVR × HVR → Cognition)
  and the LGCM a-path (CVR → HVR slope;
  [@fig-mediation]) test distinct hypotheses:
  - LME (moderation): does CVR modify how the
    HVR-cognition relationship evolves over time?
  - LGCM (mediation): does CVR affect cognition
    through hippocampal decline?
- The results diverge for CVR~mimic~:
  - LGCM a-path is null in all 6 models: age-adjusted
    CVR does not accelerate hippocampal atrophy
  - LME 3-way is significant in
    `r abs_lme$cvr_f_sig`/3 female domains
    `r if(nchar(abs_lme$cvr_f_sig_doms) > 0) sprintf("(%s)", abs_lme$cvr_f_sig_doms) else ""`
    only; `r abs_lme$cvr_m_sig`/3 in males
- This dissociation represents moderation without
  mediation through hippocampal volume: CVR~mimic~
  does not accelerate hippocampal atrophy (a-path
  null), yet it may modify how hippocampal integrity
  relates to cognitive performance over time
- This pattern is consistent with a cognitive reserve
  framework [@stern_cognitive_2012; @rabin_interactive_2018; @iadecola_framingham_2020]:
  cardiovascular risk may reduce the brain's capacity
  to maintain cognitive function at a given level of
  structural integrity, without directly driving
  structural decline
- However, the CVR~mimic~ LME evidence rests on a
  single domain in one sex. This isolated finding
  should be interpreted cautiously until replicated
- The HVR-cognition coupling (b-path) was consistent
  across CVR predictors, in line with prior LGCM
  coupling studies [@muniz-terrera_latent_2017; @bollen_latent_2006]

## Sex Differences

- Males showed `r abs_lme$cvr_m_sig`/3 significant
  CVR~mimic~ LME effects, while females showed
  `r abs_lme$cvr_f_sig`/3
  `r if(nchar(abs_lme$cvr_f_sig_doms) > 0) sprintf("(%s)", abs_lme$cvr_f_sig_doms) else ""`
- The female CVR~mimic~ Executive finding is notable
  as the only CVR~mimic~ LME effect observed in
  either sex and warrants targeted investigation in
  independent samples
- Female FRS variance compression limits
  statistical sensitivity — the FRS findings in
  females may be primarily driven by age-weighting
- Sex differences in cardiovascular risk profiles
